event: View of Piazza San Marco looking east
symbols: St. Mark's Basilica — the church built to house the relics of the Evangelist Mark, author of the second Gospel
Campanile — the bell tower calling the faithful to worship
arcade framing the view — the portal through which the sacred space is approached
description: A veduta (view painting) of Piazza San Marco in Venice, framed by the arches of a surrounding arcade in the foreground. The view looks east toward St. Mark's Basilica with its domes, and the tall Campanile (bell tower) rises prominently against a pale sky. Small figures populate the piazza. The architectural detail is precisely rendered with careful perspective. The palette is muted with warm stone tones, soft blues, and atmospheric light.
theological_description: Piazza San Marco is named for Saint Mark the Evangelist, whose relics were brought to Venice in 828 AD. The basilica built to house them became one of Christendom's greatest churches. Mark, traditionally identified as the young man who fled naked from Gethsemane (Mark 14:51-52), became Peter's interpreter and wrote the earliest Gospel, preserving the apostolic witness for all generations. The piazza that bears his name, with its gathering of faithful and pilgrims across centuries, embodies the ongoing life of the Church built upon the apostolic foundation. As Paul wrote, the Church is 'built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, with Christ Jesus himself as the cornerstone' (Eph 2:20).

scripture_refs.reason: Built on the foundation of the apostles — St. Mark's as a church honoring the apostolic evangelist
The young man at Gethsemane, traditionally identified as Mark himself, whose name this basilica bears
